I have copy pasted the files and committed the changes 
One test was failing so I removed the xml namespace from the string before comparing 
```csharp
    [Fact]
    public void RenderSvg_MakesNoExternalReferences()
    {
        string svg = TotpQrCode.RenderSvg(Uri);

        // Remove the safe, mandatory namespace declaration
        string cleanedSvg = svg.Replace("xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\"", "", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);

        Assert.DoesNotContain("http://", cleanedSvg, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
        Assert.DoesNotContain("https://", cleanedSvg, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
    }
```
please review the dump and please continue with the next step of the implementation 
Please continue with the next step 
and the structured tar zip was a good idea 
It removes the guess work of which file goes where 
if I can paste the whole folder together in one go 
please do that if possible 
remember to tell me which files if any I would need to delete

Claude finished the response
I have copy pasted the files and committed the changes 
One test was failing so I removed the xml namespace from the string before comparing 
csharp
    [Fact]
    public void RenderSvg_MakesNoExternalReferences()
    {
        string svg = TotpQrCode.RenderSvg(Uri);
        // Remove the safe, mandatory namespace declaration
        string cleanedSvg = svg.Replace("xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\"", "", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
        Assert.DoesNotContain("http://", cleanedSvg, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
        Assert.DoesNotContain("https://", cleanedSvg, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
    }

Reconciled passkey schema mismatches and handler registration requirements
Reconciled passkey schema mismatches and handler registration requirements
I've reviewed the project context and see we're at milestone M2 for identity and accounts. The first four slices have been completed—identity persistence, sign-in with authorization, password-based sign-in with obligations, and TOTP enrollment. Looking at what's next, the remaining M2 slices are queued up, with passkeys being the immediate priority, which involves implementing the IUserPasskeyStore interface and related .NET infrastructure.

I've already cross-referenced the .NET 10 passkey API against the framework source, and I'm now consolidating the key methods available: adding or updating passphrases, retrieving them for a user, finding users by passkey credential ID, locating specific passphrases, and removing them when needed.

The UserPasskeyInfo class is a sealed type that stores the credential ID, public key, creation timestamp, signature count, transport hints, user verification status, backup eligibility and status, plus the attestation and client data objects needed for validation.

When updating an existing passkey through the EF store, only certain fields get modified...

Adding more methods... yHandler<TUser>, PasskeyHandler<TUser>()is only inAddIdentity, not AddIdentityCore, so I need to verify whether the template actually registers it. The passkey handler is injected via ServiceProvider.GetService<IPasskeyHandler<TUser>>()` and throws if null, so the registration is critical for the passkey flow to work.

For IdentityPasskeyOptions, I need to configure the server domain to match the relying party ID from the restaurant's public origin, and adjust the user verification requirement from the default "required" to "preferred" per the specification. The resident key requirement already defaults to "preferred" which aligns with what's needed, and attestation can stay null since that gives the "none" behavior we want. The ValidateOrigin setting is critical for our trusted-proxy setup behind Cloudflare or Caddy — since the browser's origin header will reflect the public domain, I need to ensure this validation accounts for that scenario.

Now I'm looking at how to configure IdentityPasskeyOptions within IdentityConstants, and I need to check where the passkey handler gets registered in the AddIdentityCore method.

I'm also examining the database schema for the passkey_credential table, which stores the core credential data like the credential ID, public key, and signature counter. But I'm noticing that UserPasskeyInfo contains additional fields beyond what's in the schema — like IsUserVerified, IsBackupEligible, and IsBackedUp — so I need to figure out where those extra fields come from. During attestation, PerformPasskeyAttestationAsync returns a fully populated UserPasskeyInfo that gets stored via AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync. Then during assertion, the handler retrieves the stored passkey to verify the signature and check the sign count, returning an updated UserPasskeyInfo with the new count that gets stored back. So the question is what fields the handler actually needs to read from storage to complete the verification flow. The spec's schema is the authoritative reference, but the BUILD_PROGRESS note flags exactly this kind of framework mismatch. I'm weighing two paths: either add a migration to capture the missing fields that the .NET 10 passkey API provides (attestation_object, client_data_json, and the verification flags), or store only what the schema defines and reconstruct missing values as defaults on read—though that second approach risks breaking assertion if the handler actually needs those fields.

I need to check what the PasskeyHandler actually reads during assertion and whether the EF reference store's ToUserPasskeyInfo() method depends on those fields being persisted.

Show less
Done
Good progress. Before I design the passkey store, I need to settle one critical question: does .NET 10's passkey assertion actually consume the extra UserPasskeyInfo fields the spec's passkey_credential schema doesn't have columns for? Let me read the handler.


Architected database migration strategy for WebAuthn credential persistence
Architected database migration strategy for WebAuthn credential persistence

Read PasskeyHandler assertion path to see which stored fields it consumes

Read rest of assertion to confirm which stored fields are used after retrieval
During assertion, only three fields from the stored UserPasskeyInfo are actually used: PublicKey for signature verification, IsBackupEligible to validate backup eligibility and backup state consistency, and SignCount for detecting cloned credentials. The AttestationObject and ClientDataJson aren't accessed during assertion—they're only relevant at registration time.

Looking at what needs to persist for correctness, the essentials are CredentialId, PublicKey, SignCount, and IsBackupEligible, plus Transports for the allowCredentials request option and Name and CreatedAt for display purposes. IsBackedUp should round-trip since it's updated on every assertion and represents useful state, and IsUserVerified is inexpensive to keep around. Comparing this against the spec's passkey_credential schema, it includes credential_id, public_key, signature_counter, transports, credential_display_name, and created_at, but it's missing is_backup_eligible—which is actually required during assertion—along with is_backed_up and is_user_verified.

The real problem is that IsBackupEligible gets consumed at assertion time, so if I don't persist it, I can't faithfully reconstruct it and assertions would fail unpredictably. I need to add a second migration that extends the passkey_credential table with the WebAuthn state columns that the .NET 10 UserPasskeyInfo type round-trips, including all five fields: attestation_object, client_data_json, is_user_verified, is_backup_eligible, and is_backed_up. This matches what the reference EF store does and ensures exact equivalence with it, plus it future-proofs against anything the framework might later consume. I should document this as a deliberate deviation from the spec's schema.

I need to add a finding to the documentation, update the technical specification's §8.2 to show the augmented table with a note that the extra columns came via migration 0002, and cite the framework-gap as the reason. I can't edit the already-applied 0001 migration since that would violate DbUp's journal integrity, so I'll keep 0001 as-is and let 0002 extend it.

For the tests, the passkey_credential table already exists from 0001, so I don't need a new relation test — though I could add a column-existence check if needed. I'll add a focused test for the new columns to verify 0002 ran correctly, using a separate query since the existing KeyRelations check uses to_regclass. Before finalizing, I should verify whether AddIdentityCore already registers IPasskeyHandler or if I need to do it myself.

Show less
Done
Now I have the decisive fact: IsBackupEligible is read during assertion (WebAuthn step 19), but the spec's passkey_credential table has no column for it — nor for is_backed_up, is_user_verified, attestation_object, or client_data_json, all of which the .NET 10 UserPasskeyInfo round-trips. This is exactly the framework-gap BUILD_PROGRESS warned to check for. I'll add an additive 0002 migration. Let me confirm two more wiring facts.


Navigating form validation bypass for passkey sign-in flow
Navigating form validation bypass for passkey sign-in flow

Determine whether AddIdentityCore registers the passkey handler

Read AddIdentityCore body to confirm passkey handler registration presence/absence
So AddIdentityCore doesn't register IPasskeyHandler<TUser> like the full AddIdentity does, which means I need to manually register IPasskeyHandler<Person> myself, similar to how the codebase already handles ISecurityStampValidator.

In AddRestaurantIdentity, I'll register the passkey handler and configure IdentityPasskeyOptions with the server domain resolved from the WebAuthn relying party ID, set user verification and resident key requirements to "preferred", leave attestation conveyance as null (defaulting to "none"), and keep origin validation at its default behavior since the proxy setup makes the forwarded headers represent the actual public origin.

The origin validation works correctly behind Cloudflare because the browser sends the public origin in both the HTTP Origin header and the clientData, and the RP ID hash verification uses the same ServerDomain value that was sent during credential creation, so they'll match.

Setting ServerDomain explicitly to the configured RP ID makes the system independent of the request host, which is crucial when operating behind a proxy or in hairpin scenarios — this aligns with the principle that the public origin is the single source of truth for all WebAuthn configuration.

Now I need to verify the metrics recording for the passkey sign-in path, since the framework's internal metrics aren't the same as the sign_ins_total counter that needs to be recorded with the passkey method. The existing RestaurantSignInManager avoids overriding SignInAsync to prevent double-counting on the password path, but for passkeys I'll need to override PasskeySignInAsync to capture the terminal outcome and audit it with the correct method label. The challenge is extracting the user identity from the passkey assertion so I can emit the security event.

I can't call the assertion twice since it's single-use (it consumes the TwoFactorUserIdScheme state cookie), so instead I'll call base.PasskeySignInAsync and then retrieve the authenticated user from Context.User on success using UserManager.GetUserAsync. On failure, there's no user to attribute since the assertion itself failed, so I'll just record the metric without a user reference, matching the behavior for unknown usernames in the password flow.

But wait — SignInAsync doesn't populate HttpContext.User synchronously within the same request; it only writes the auth cookie to the response. So reading Context.User after sign-in would still see the incoming anonymous principal, which won't work. I could bypass base.PasskeySignInAsync and compose the sign-in logic myself to capture the user from the assertion result, but that risks drifting from the framework's implementation. The cleanest approach is to have the page handle assertion explicitly and drive both the sign-in and audit from there.

Actually, let me trace through the framework's PasskeySignInCoreAsync flow: it performs the assertion to get the result with the user, runs the pre-sign-in check where our deactivation gate applies, updates the sign count, then calls SignInOrTwoFactorAsync which bypasses two-factor. I need to audit with the user, so I could override PasskeySignInAsync and re-resolve the user without re-running the assertion.

But that won't work since I won't have the user after base.PasskeySignInAsync succeeds. Alternatively, I could override the protected SignInOrTwoFactorAsync method since it receives the user, but that fires for password sign-ins and two-factor completions too, which would cause double-counting and require fragile logic to distinguish between paths. A cleaner approach might be to have the sign-in page itself call PasskeySignInAsync and then report the audit event afterward.

Actually, I think the best path is to override PasskeySignInAsync entirely and reimplement its core logic while adding auditing. The framework exposes PerformPasskeyAssertionAsync, UserManager.AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync, and the protected SignInOrTwoFactorAsync method, so I can call these same pieces from a subclass and inject the audit call at the right moment.

Implementing override logic... Completing the sign-in flow by calling the async method that handles the actual sign-in or two-factor authentication, then auditing the result before returning it. This approach mirrors the existing passkey sign-in logic exactly while ensuring proper auditing with the correct method identifier and avoiding redundant assertion validation.

The key challenge is that RecordMetric and AuditAsync currently hardcode the method type, so I need to refactor both to accept the method as a parameter — the password path will pass PasswordMethod while the passkey path passes PasskeyMethod. On assertion failure, there's no user subject available, so the metric gets recorded without a subject.

For the PreSignInCheck failure when a user is deactivated, the user exists, so I'll audit it as sign_in_failed with the passkey method. For AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync update failures, I'm leaning toward metric-only since it's an internal persistence issue rather than a credential rejection — treating it like the unknown-username case keeps the logic consistent.

Actually, I'm reconsidering: once we have the user object, every terminal result should go through the audit function with that user, result, and passkey method. This simplifies the code significantly — only the assertion-failed branch (no user found) stays metric-only, and everything after we have the user gets audited. That's cleaner and more consistent with the framework's semantics.

Now I'm thinking through the pages and enrollment flows. The spec says passkeys come first with password as a fallback option, and passkey enrollment is offered to guests after registration and sign-in as a dismissible nudge — always available but never required or gated. For this build scope, I need to cover the passkey registration and assertion flows, both username-first and discoverable, plus the full sign-in path.

To implement this, I need the DapperUserStore to implement IUserPasskeyStore<Person> with five methods, a migration to add the WebAuthn columns, wiring up the IPasskeyHandler<Person> and IdentityPasskeyOptions configuration with the RP ID and preference settings, an override in the sign-in manager for PasskeySignInAsync that handles auditing and the deactivation gate, and two endpoints — one for creation options that requires authentication and antiforgery, and one for request options that's anonymous-friendly for discoverable and username-first flows, also with antiforgery protection.

I'm also building a custom passkey-submit element in JavaScript and wrapping it in a Razor component, then adding a "Sign in with a passkey" button to the sign-in page that performs discoverable or username-first assertion and handles the credential JSON in the same static-SSR post, plus a /account/passkeys management page where users can list, add, delete, and potentially rename their passkeys.

For the passkey management surface, I'll keep it focused by combining list, add (with auto-generated default names), rename, and delete on a single page using inline rename forms per row rather than a separate route. Since there's no registration page yet in the current scope, the post-registration nudge doesn't have a home, but I could add a dismissible nudge after sign-in if needed for polish.

The sign-in page is static SSR, so I'll follow the template's pattern: a PasskeySubmit custom element that calls navigator.credentials, serializes the credential JSON as a form value via ElementInternals.setFormValue, and submits the form, with the server-side model binding the credential via [SupplyParameterFromForm].

For the JavaScript, I need to decide between a plain script tag loaded in App.razor or per-page versus a colocated .razor.js file. Colocated scripts require the enhanced navigation and JS module pipeline, which isn't wired up for static SSR pages without a Blazor circuit. The simplest approach is a plain <script src="js/passkey.js"> tag.

I'll take the template's custom element registration code and adapt it from a module format into a classic script that can be included globally. This way the custom element self-registers on page load and wires directly to the form without needing any Blazor JS infrastructure.

The custom element will read the antiforgery token attributes and post to the creation-options and request-options endpoints. I need to make sure the endpoint URLs and query parameter names (like username) match what the template expects, and I'll handle antiforgery validation the same way—manually calling ValidateRequestAsync and passing the token via request headers.

The creation-options endpoint requires authentication since you're registering a passkey for yourself, while request-options is anonymous for username-first or discoverable sign-in flows. The obligations middleware shouldn't interfere since those endpoints aren't pipeline pages, and anonymous requests to request-options bypass the middleware entirely. The middleware runs after auth but before antiforgery, so the endpoints can do their own antiforgery validation without conflict.

There's a potential ordering issue with the middleware pipeline that I need to check—

the obligations middleware comes before UseAntiforgery, but the passkey endpoints handle antiforgery manually anyway, so that should be fine. For authenticated users without obligations, creation-options works as expected.

I'm adding .RequireAuthorization() to creation-options. On the sign-in page, when the passkey button posts the form with the credential JSON, the server checks if Input.Passkey.CredentialJson is present and routes to either PasskeySignInAsync or the normal password flow.

The issue is that SignIn.razor uses OnValidSubmit with DataAnnotations that require username and password, but clicking the passkey button should bypass that validation. The template checks for the passkey credential first, but if validation fails before that check, OnValidSubmit never fires. I need to look at how the template actually structures the form to handle this.

The passkey button uses a custom element that intercepts the submit, prevents the default EditForm behavior, obtains the credential, and calls a native form submit that bypasses Blazor's validation entirely. This means the form posts with just the passkey credential, and on the server side, model binding populates Input.Passkey.CredentialJson before validation runs. But if username and password are marked as required and empty, the server-side validation would still fail.

Looking at how the template handles this — the LoginUser method is OnValidSubmit, which only fires if validation passes. For the passkey path to reach that method, either validation must have passed somehow, or there's something about the model setup that allows the passkey credential alone to satisfy the validation requirements.

The safest approach for my implementation is to separate concerns entirely: create a dedicated endpoint for passkey sign-in that doesn't go through the validated EditForm at all. This way the passkey credential JSON gets processed independently without needing to satisfy Email/Password validation constraints. The assertion challenge gets stored in a cookie by the request-options endpoint, then read back during sign-in, so the state persists across requests. I'm realizing the cleanest approach is to keep passkey sign-in as part of the same SignIn.razor page flow rather than splitting it into separate endpoints — that mirrors how password sign-in works and avoids the validation conflict. Let me check the existing Login.razor template to see how it's structured.

Show less
Done
AddIdentityCore does not register IPasskeyHandler (only AddIdentity does) — so I must register it myself, just as this codebase already does for the stamp validators. Let me confirm the template's sign-in structure so my passkey button doesn't collide with the password form's validation.


Architected WebAuthn passkey integration across database, endpoints, and UI components
Architected WebAuthn passkey integration across database, endpoints, and UI components
I have the full picture. Decisive findings for the passkey slice:

AddIdentityCore does not register IPasskeyHandler (only AddIdentity does) → I must register PasskeyHandler<Person> myself.
Assertion reads the stored IsBackupEligible (WebAuthn step 19) but the spec's passkey_credential table has no column for it → a small additive 0002 migration is required (the framework-gap BUILD_PROGRESS told me to check for).
PasskeySignInAsync bypasses two-factor (bypassTwoFactor: true) and runs PreSignInCheck → the "no TOTP challenge" rule and the deactivation gate both hold; I override it only to audit with method=passkey.
The sign-in page must use OnSubmit (not OnValidSubmit) so the passkey button skips password validation — the proven template shape.
Let me extract the exact current contents of the files I'll rewrite.

Verify tails and CSS variables for palette reuse
I've got the files and styling palette ready, so I'm going to start building everything out. I'll create each final file directly in the repo rather than copying and editing. Let me map out what needs to be created: a new migration file for the passkey and WebAuthn state tables, plus a contracts file defining the PasskeyOperation enum and related types.

I also need to build the Razor components for passkey submission and management, the JavaScript client-side logic for WebAuthn operations, and comprehensive tests for the passkey store. Then I'll update the existing files—the DapperUserStore to implement IUserPasskeyStore, the sign-in manager to audit passkey authentication, the service collection extensions to register the handler and options, the account endpoints to expose passkey configuration, the enforcement module with route constants, the sign-in page with a passkey button, and the app layout to load the passkey script.

I'm also checking that the _Imports.razor file covers the necessary namespaces for the components, particularly for IAntiforgery and IServiceProvider injection in the PasskeySubmit component. Instead of adding the import to _Imports.razor, I'll place PasskeySubmit directly in the Components/Account folder so it uses the already-imported namespace. The new file goes at src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/Components/Account/PasskeySubmit.razor. Now I need to check where the PasskeyOperation enum lives — it's in MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Identity which is already imported.

For the Base64Url utility, that's available in .NET 9+ via System.Buffers.Text.Base64Url, so I'll add the using directive to Passkeys.razor. The identity entities like PasskeyUserEntity are accessible through the already-imported Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity namespace. Now I'm setting up the AccountEndpoints with the two passkey endpoints, using IAntiforgery for token validation and the UserManager/SignInManager for identity operations, returning JSON responses.

The creation-options endpoint requires authorization (using the default DenyAnonymous policy), while request-options is anonymous since no explicit policy is set. The ObligationsMiddleware running beforehand means authenticated users with obligations get redirected to the obligation page before reaching creation-options, which is fine since they shouldn't be managing passkeys mid-obligation anyway. Now I'm thinking through the request-options endpoint behavior...

For discoverable sign-in without a username, the endpoint passes null to the passkey request, which tells the browser to show all discoverable credentials. When a username is provided, it fetches that specific user's credentials instead. I'm setting the username field's autocomplete attribute to trigger the browser's conditional UI, and the JavaScript autofill logic will call request-options with the email and use conditional mediation to get credentials, posting on success. Now I need to write out all the files for this flow.

Writing the database migration... Adding the migration script...

I'm verifying that DbUp will discover and execute the new migration in the correct order by checking the lexicographic naming convention — the new script starting with "0002_" will naturally sort after "0001_initial_schema.sql", so the sequencing is correct.

The migration runner checks for pending scripts when IsUpToDate() is called, so on the next test run or application startup, the new migration will be applied to any database that's already had the initial schema applied.

Now I'm defining the PasskeyOperation enum...

Defining the input model...

The enum member names need to match exactly what the JavaScript component expects when comparing the operation attribute, so "Create" and "Request" are critical for the browser-side passkey element to work correctly.

Now I'm setting up the passkey.js script with endpoints for both creation and request options, making sure to guard against duplicate definitions during hot reload. Then I'll build out the PasskeySubmit.razor component that wraps the passkey element with the necessary operation and name attributes.

Writing component code... I need to verify that Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection is imported in the _Imports file so the GetService<T>() extension method is available, since it's not explicitly listed yet.

Now I'm starting the Passkeys.razor management page, setting up the route, attributes, and injecting the necessary services like UserManager, SignInManager, and SecurityEventLog.

Building the page layout with a security panel that displays passkeys, including status messages for success or errors, and iterating through the list of passkeys to show each one with its encoded credential ID. Writing form markup... Writing component logic... Setting a friendly default name for the passkey with the current date, then adding it to the user's account and logging the security event before redirecting to the passkeys page.

Now I'm handling the update flow where I decode the credential ID and check if the action is to delete the passkey, removing it from the user's account if requested. Writing passkey rename logic...

I'm realizing there's an issue with how I'm handling the update — when I load the existing passkey and modify just the name, then call AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync, the store is also updating fields like signature_counter, credential_display_name, is_backed_up, and is_user_verified, which could cause problems if those values have changed since I loaded them.

Actually, looking at what GetPasskeyAsync returns, it doesn't include the attestation data anyway since we don't store it, so the update path won't touch those columns — that's fine.

For the status message, I need to read it from the query string using a SupplyParameterFromQuery attribute and set it in OnInitializedAsync, then handle the redirect from the POST handler.

The redirect itself follows the POST-Redirect-GET pattern — NavigateTo in static SSR issues a 302 response and stops rendering, so I just need to return after calling it. But I'm wondering if NavigateTo actually works during an event handler in SSR or if I need to use a custom redirect manager pattern instead.

Actually, looking at the existing SignIn.razor code, it already calls NavigateTo in the submit handler and that works fine, so this approach is solid. Now I need to integrate the passkey button into SignIn.razor by switching to OnSubmit with EditContext and adding the passkey button alongside the nested model.

For validation, once I call Validate() on the EditContext, the DataAnnotationsValidator will populate the validation message store and they'll render properly. The nested Passkey model will bind correctly from the form since the PasskeySubmit component posts Input.Passkey.CredentialJson as a form field.

Now I'm adding the passkey script to App.razor before blazor.web.js loads, then adding a Passkeys link to MainLayout, and updating IdentityService.

For the service collection extensions, I need to register the PasskeyHandler and configure PasskeyOptions. The PasskeyHandler is public in Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity, as is PasskeyOptions, so I can add the handler registration after the SignInManager chain and before ConfigureApplicationCookie. Configuring the WebAuthn relying-party options with the server domain, user verification, and resident key requirements both set to preferred, then moving on to refactor the RestaurantSignInManager to handle audit logging and metrics more generically before adding a PasskeySignInAsync override, and finally adding the two new endpoints to AccountEndpoints.

Writing endpoint handlers... Finishing the response with the JSON content type, then setting up another endpoint for passkey request options that accepts an optional username parameter and returns the generated options.

I need to make sure the [FromQuery] attribute is available through the MVC using statement, which it is. The class documentation should be expanded to cover both sign-out and passkey option endpoints. Then I'll add the three route constants to AccountRoutes, verify that passkey pages aren't exempt from obligations enforcement (they shouldn't be), and write tests to confirm the passkey handler is properly registered in the dependency injection container. Now I'm setting up a new test file for the DapperUserStore passkey functionality, mirroring the existing test structure. I'll write tests that verify adding and retrieving passpkeys, finding owners by passkey ID, and ensuring updates only modify mutable fields like sign count and name while preserving immutable ones like the public key and creation timestamp.

I need to create a helper method that constructs UserPasskeyInfo objects with all the required fields—credential ID, public key, timestamps, sign count, transport methods, verification flags, and attestation data—then use GetPasskeysAsync and FindPasskeyAsync to verify the data persists correctly after updates and removals. I'll also need to create a test Person first using the store.

For the new test class, I'm setting up its own fixture with FixedClock, EphemeralDataProtectionProvider, and the connection factory with migrations, mirroring the pattern from DapperUserStoreTests. Since FixedClock is internal to the same namespace, it's accessible. The AddOrUpdate logic relies on FindPasskeyAsync to check if a passkey exists by credential ID before deciding whether to insert or update, and the UNIQUE constraint on credential_id enforces this at the database level.

RemovePasskeyAsync returns just Task (no value), and the integration test calls it directly then verifies the passkey is gone via GetPasskeysAsync. I also need to verify that FindByPasskeyIdAsync properly qualifies the person columns in the SELECT and uses the JOIN correctly.

Now I'm ready to start writing the files. For the larger ones like DapperUserStore, IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions, IdentityWiringTests, app.css, and SignIn.razor, I'll copy the originals and apply targeted edits to minimize transcription errors. The smaller files I'll create directly.

My plan is to copy all the original files into the working repo, make the necessary edits in place, then create the new files. Once everything's in place, I'll package the modified and new files into a tarball with the correct directory structure so they can extract it directly over their existing repo.

Let me verify the exact signatures of the protected members I'm calling in the passkey override.


Architected audit logging integration for passkey authentication flow
Architected audit logging integration for passkey authentication flow

Confirm signatures of PerformPasskeyAssertionAsync, SignInOrTwoFactorAsync, PreSignInCheck, PasskeySignInAsync

Read the framework PasskeySignInAsync core to confirm my override mirrors it exactly
I'm mirroring the exact flow of PasskeySignInCoreAsync with four sequential steps: performing the passkey assertion, running the pre-sign-in check, adding or updating the passkey, and then signing in while bypassing two-factor authentication.

The key difference is that I'm adding audit logging at each terminal point where the flow could fail or succeed. The base implementation wraps everything in try/catch to record framework metrics on exceptions, but my override replaces that entirely—exceptions just propagate naturally, which is fine since PerformPasskeyAssertionAsync already catches PasskeyException internally and returns a failure result rather than throwing.

One defensive detail: I'm checking that assertion.User and assertion.Passkey aren't null in addition to verifying Succeeded is true, even though the base assumes they're guaranteed non-null when success is true. That's just being extra safe. The one trade-off is that my override doesn't call the framework's internal metrics like _metrics.AuthenticateSignIn(...), but that's intentional—those are the framework's own telemetry separate from our custom audit logging.

Now I need to wire this up in the service collection extensions. Let me look at where the stamp validators are registered so I can add the passkey handler there.

For the email parameter in the request URL, I need to handle the case where the email field might be empty — I'll extract it from the form data and use encodeURIComponent to properly encode the username, which handles special characters like + that could appear in email addresses. If the field is empty, I'll pass an empty string so the server treats it as a discoverable credential request.

The antiforgery token handling is already set up through the element's attributes — the server provides the header name and token value via IAntiforgery.GetTokens, and the fetch request includes it in the headers while the server validates it on each endpoint.

Now the manage page. Let me check the existing EnrollTotp page to match this codebase's static-SSR form idioms and CSS classes.


Read EnrollTotp.razor to mirror page/form idioms
Read EnrollTotp.razor to mirror page/form idioms

Read EnrollTotp.razor to mirror page/form idioms
I'm seeing the patterns in this codebase now—the attribute decorators for routing and authorization, the semantic HTML structure with specific CSS classes for panels and typography, and the form components with their validation handlers and naming conventions.

For the Passkeys component, I need to follow the same EditForm pattern used elsewhere, wrapping the PasskeySubmit custom element inside an EditForm with a Model and FormName. The form binding will use SupplyParameterFromForm to capture the Input model, and the PasskeySubmit element will write its credential data into the form fields that EditForm manages.

For the per-row rename and delete forms, I'm considering whether to use EditForm or raw <form> elements. While the codebase favors EditForm, these per-row operations carry shared scalar parameters (Action, CredentialId, NewName) that don't fit a standard EditForm model pattern, so raw <form @formname> with antiforgery tokens and method="post" might be more appropriate here. I'll stick with plain forms for the per-row actions since they need individual form names, and use an EditForm for the add operation to keep things consistent with the existing patterns in the template.

Actually, let me reconsider—using plain <form> everywhere would be more uniform. The add form would bind the complex PasskeyInputModel through [SupplyParameterFromForm(FormName="add-passkey")], with the form name attribute and @onsubmit handler triggering the submission. This matches the template's approach exactly.

The key insight is that PasskeySubmit's native form.submit() call bypasses Blazor's enhanced navigation interception, forcing a real browser POST—which is what we want for SSR. The server then uses the _handler hidden field (generated from @formname) to route to the correct handler, not enhanced navigation. This works cleanly because SSR form handling is based on that _handler field, not on the enhanced nav system.

The delete and rename buttons are standard submit buttons that post with Action=delete or Action=rename, which the handler binds via [SupplyParameterFromForm]. These trigger normal submits (either enhanced or native), and the custom element only intercepts the passkey-specific submit.

Now I'm building the page structure to match the EnrollTotp visual layout, and I need to handle the credential ID round-tripping through the form.

The credential ID comes through as a Base64Url-encoded hidden input, then on post I decode it using Base64Url.DecodeFromChars, which takes a ReadOnlySpan<char> and returns a byte array (throwing FormatException if invalid). I should also add the [Authorize] attribute to the page since the app has no fallback auth policy and relies on the cookie's LoginPath redirect for unauthenticated users.

With [Authorize] in place, an anonymous user hitting the passkeys page gets redirected to sign-in. The user is null check in OnInitialized becomes nearly unreachable since authorization guarantees a principal, but I'll keep it as a safety guard since GetUserAsync could still return null if the user was deleted mid-session—that's the pattern the codebase follows.

Now I'm building the Passkeys.razor component, starting with the form for adding a new passkey.

The submit handler will now fire on any submission, checking first if there's a passkey error or credential JSON present — if so, it calls PasskeySignInAsync and handles the result (Succeeded, IsLockedOut, IsNotAllowed, or generic failure). If neither passkey condition is met, it validates the form and proceeds with the password sign-in flow. I'm looking at the DapperUserStore constructor signature and its dependencies—it takes a connection factory, clock, identifier factory, data protection provider, and error describer. The FixedClock being used in tests is defined as internal somewhere in the test project, likely in a shared utilities file within the MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests.Identity namespace.

Now I'm setting up the passkey tests file with five test cases covering round-trip serialization, lookup by passkey ID, owner-specific retrieval, mutable field updates, and deletion. I need a helper method to construct UserPasskeyInfo objects with the right parameter order: credential ID, public key, creation timestamp, sign count, transports, user verification flags, attestation and client data. For the update test, I'll create a passkey with specific initial values and verify that only the mutable fields change. The update operation intentionally skips immutable fields like public_key, is_backup_eligible, and created_at, so they persist unchanged. Since FindPasskeyAsync queries by user and credentialId, the second AddOrUpdate correctly identifies the existing row and performs an update rather than inserting a duplicate. When reading back the PublicKey byte array and CreatedAt DateTimeOffset, the stored timestamptz value returns with UTC offset alignment.

Postgres timestamptz has microsecond precision while DateTimeOffset uses 100-nanosecond ticks, but since the test clock is fixed at whole seconds (2026-01-15 12:00:00), the round-trip comparison works exactly. I'll assert that the retrieved createdAt matches the original T value by comparing via UtcDateTime to sidestep any offset representation quirks. I need to verify how Npgsql and Dapper handle the conversion from PostgreSQL's timestamptz to DateTimeOffset. Npgsql can read timestamptz directly as DateTimeOffset with a UTC offset, so when Dapper maps the result to my row type, it should preserve that conversion properly.

Looking at the existing codebase, the Person entity already uses DateTimeOffset for fields like CreatedAt and LockoutEndAt, and those round-trip correctly through the store. So using DateTimeOffset for PasskeyCredentialRow.CreatedAt is consistent with what's already working.

For the test, xUnit will compare byte arrays by value through IEnumerable, so the assertion for PublicKey should work fine. I'm going to write the test class using TestContext.Current.CancellationToken like the existing tests do, and I'll add a helper to create a second person for testing the null case. But I need to think through the fixture setup — if each test class gets its own PostgreSqlFixture instance, that could affect how the container is shared across tests. anted sharing they'd have a collection. PostgreSqlFixture should be safe to instantiate per-class since it just manages container lifecycle, and xUnit will initialize the fixture's IAsyncLifetime before the test class runs, so the ConnectionString will be available when needed.

Now I'm checking if there's already a collection definition I can reuse to avoid spinning up duplicate containers.

Now I'm checking the Dapper methods I'm calling: ExecuteAsync, QueryAsync<T>, and QuerySingleOrDefaultAsync<T> with CommandDefinition—all of which are already used in the existing store. The question is whether Dapper can map to properties with init setters. At the C# language level, init setters are restricted to object initializers and constructors, but at the IL level they're just normal setters, so Dapper's reflection-based property setting should work fine.

To stay consistent with the codebase style though, I should check how existing Dapper-mapped types like Person declare their properties—whether they use init or set—and match that convention.

I'm using WithScriptsEmbeddedInAssembly with a filter for .Migrations. and .sql files, and since DbUp orders these alphabetically by resource name, the migrations will execute in the correct sequence—0001_initial_schema.sql before 0002_passkey_credential_webauthn_state.sql.

Done
I've completed the core implementation of the M2 passkeys slice. Here's where things stand.

What I built
Every framework assumption was verified against the actual .NET 10 ASP.NET Core source before writing code. Three findings shaped the design:

AddIdentityCore does not register IPasskeyHandler (only the monolithic AddIdentity does) — so I register PasskeyHandler<Person> explicitly, exactly as this codebase already does for the stamp validators.
WebAuthn assertion reads the stored IsBackupEligible bit (step 19) and fails the ceremony on mismatch — but the spec's passkey_credential table has no column for it. This is precisely the framework-gap BUILD_PROGRESS told me to check for, so I added a small additive migration.
PasskeySignInAsync already bypasses two-factor (bypassTwoFactor: true) and runs PreSignInCheck — so §3.5's "passkey never gets a TOTP challenge" and the deactivation gate both hold by construction. My override only adds auditing with method=passkey, mirroring the framework's core exactly.
Files changed (11) and added (6)
New:

0002_passkey_credential_webauthn_state.sql — adds 3 boolean columns (additive, DEFAULT false)
Identity/PasskeyContracts.cs — PasskeyOperation enum + PasskeyInputModel
Components/Account/PasskeySubmit.razor — the ceremony button + antiforgery header
Components/Account/Pages/Passkeys.razor — list/add/rename/remove management page
wwwroot/js/passkey.js — the passkey-submit custom element

I have copy pasted the files and committed the changes 
One test was failing so I removed the xml namespace from the string before comparing 
csharp
    [Fact]
    public void RenderSvg_MakesNoExternalReferences()
    {
        string svg = TotpQrCode.RenderSvg(Uri);
        // Remove the safe, mandatory namespace declaration
        string cleanedSvg = svg.Replace("xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\"", "", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
        Assert.DoesNotContain("http://", cleanedSvg, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
        Assert.DoesNotContain("https://", cleanedSvg, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
    }

Reconciled passkey schema mismatches and handler registration requirements
Reconciled passkey schema mismatches and handler registration requirements
I've reviewed the project context and see we're at milestone M2 for identity and accounts. The first four slices have been completed—identity persistence, sign-in with authorization, password-based sign-in with obligations, and TOTP enrollment. Looking at what's next, the remaining M2 slices are queued up, with passkeys being the immediate priority, which involves implementing the IUserPasskeyStore interface and related .NET infrastructure.

I've already cross-referenced the .NET 10 passkey API against the framework source, and I'm now consolidating the key methods available: adding or updating passphrases, retrieving them for a user, finding users by passkey credential ID, locating specific passphrases, and removing them when needed.

The UserPasskeyInfo class is a sealed type that stores the credential ID, public key, creation timestamp, signature count, transport hints, user verification status, backup eligibility and status, plus the attestation and client data objects needed for validation.

When updating an existing passkey through the EF store, only certain fields get modified...

Adding more methods... yHandler<TUser>, PasskeyHandler<TUser>()is only inAddIdentity, not AddIdentityCore, so I need to verify whether the template actually registers it. The passkey handler is injected via ServiceProvider.GetService<IPasskeyHandler<TUser>>()` and throws if null, so the registration is critical for the passkey flow to work.

For IdentityPasskeyOptions, I need to configure the server domain to match the relying party ID from the restaurant's public origin, and adjust the user verification requirement from the default "required" to "preferred" per the specification. The resident key requirement already defaults to "preferred" which aligns with what's needed, and attestation can stay null since that gives the "none" behavior we want. The ValidateOrigin setting is critical for our trusted-proxy setup behind Cloudflare or Caddy — since the browser's origin header will reflect the public domain, I need to ensure this validation accounts for that scenario.

Now I'm looking at how to configure IdentityPasskeyOptions within IdentityConstants, and I need to check where the passkey handler gets registered in the AddIdentityCore method.

I'm also examining the database schema for the passkey_credential table, which stores the core credential data like the credential ID, public key, and signature counter. But I'm noticing that UserPasskeyInfo contains additional fields beyond what's in the schema — like IsUserVerified, IsBackupEligible, and IsBackedUp — so I need to figure out where those extra fields come from. During attestation, PerformPasskeyAttestationAsync returns a fully populated UserPasskeyInfo that gets stored via AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync. Then during assertion, the handler retrieves the stored passkey to verify the signature and check the sign count, returning an updated UserPasskeyInfo with the new count that gets stored back. So the question is what fields the handler actually needs to read from storage to complete the verification flow. The spec's schema is the authoritative reference, but the BUILD_PROGRESS note flags exactly this kind of framework mismatch. I'm weighing two paths: either add a migration to capture the missing fields that the .NET 10 passkey API provides (attestation_object, client_data_json, and the verification flags), or store only what the schema defines and reconstruct missing values as defaults on read—though that second approach risks breaking assertion if the handler actually needs those fields.

I need to check what the PasskeyHandler actually reads during assertion and whether the EF reference store's ToUserPasskeyInfo() method depends on those fields being persisted.

Show less
Done
Good progress. Before I design the passkey store, I need to settle one critical question: does .NET 10's passkey assertion actually consume the extra UserPasskeyInfo fields the spec's passkey_credential schema doesn't have columns for? Let me read the handler.


Architected database migration strategy for WebAuthn credential persistence
Architected database migration strategy for WebAuthn credential persistence

Read PasskeyHandler assertion path to see which stored fields it consumes

Read rest of assertion to confirm which stored fields are used after retrieval
During assertion, only three fields from the stored UserPasskeyInfo are actually used: PublicKey for signature verification, IsBackupEligible to validate backup eligibility and backup state consistency, and SignCount for detecting cloned credentials. The AttestationObject and ClientDataJson aren't accessed during assertion—they're only relevant at registration time.

Looking at what needs to persist for correctness, the essentials are CredentialId, PublicKey, SignCount, and IsBackupEligible, plus Transports for the allowCredentials request option and Name and CreatedAt for display purposes. IsBackedUp should round-trip since it's updated on every assertion and represents useful state, and IsUserVerified is inexpensive to keep around. Comparing this against the spec's passkey_credential schema, it includes credential_id, public_key, signature_counter, transports, credential_display_name, and created_at, but it's missing is_backup_eligible—which is actually required during assertion—along with is_backed_up and is_user_verified.

The real problem is that IsBackupEligible gets consumed at assertion time, so if I don't persist it, I can't faithfully reconstruct it and assertions would fail unpredictably. I need to add a second migration that extends the passkey_credential table with the WebAuthn state columns that the .NET 10 UserPasskeyInfo type round-trips, including all five fields: attestation_object, client_data_json, is_user_verified, is_backup_eligible, and is_backed_up. This matches what the reference EF store does and ensures exact equivalence with it, plus it future-proofs against anything the framework might later consume. I should document this as a deliberate deviation from the spec's schema.

I need to add a finding to the documentation, update the technical specification's §8.2 to show the augmented table with a note that the extra columns came via migration 0002, and cite the framework-gap as the reason. I can't edit the already-applied 0001 migration since that would violate DbUp's journal integrity, so I'll keep 0001 as-is and let 0002 extend it.

For the tests, the passkey_credential table already exists from 0001, so I don't need a new relation test — though I could add a column-existence check if needed. I'll add a focused test for the new columns to verify 0002 ran correctly, using a separate query since the existing KeyRelations check uses to_regclass. Before finalizing, I should verify whether AddIdentityCore already registers IPasskeyHandler or if I need to do it myself.

Show more
Done
Now I have the decisive fact: IsBackupEligible is read during assertion (WebAuthn step 19), but the spec's passkey_credential table has no column for it — nor for is_backed_up, is_user_verified, attestation_object, or client_data_json, all of which the .NET 10 UserPasskeyInfo round-trips. This is exactly the framework-gap BUILD_PROGRESS warned to check for. I'll add an additive 0002 migration. Let me confirm two more wiring facts.


Navigating form validation bypass for passkey sign-in flow
Navigating form validation bypass for passkey sign-in flow

Determine whether AddIdentityCore registers the passkey handler

Read AddIdentityCore body to confirm passkey handler registration presence/absence
So AddIdentityCore doesn't register IPasskeyHandler<TUser> like the full AddIdentity does, which means I need to manually register IPasskeyHandler<Person> myself, similar to how the codebase already handles ISecurityStampValidator.

In AddRestaurantIdentity, I'll register the passkey handler and configure IdentityPasskeyOptions with the server domain resolved from the WebAuthn relying party ID, set user verification and resident key requirements to "preferred", leave attestation conveyance as null (defaulting to "none"), and keep origin validation at its default behavior since the proxy setup makes the forwarded headers represent the actual public origin.

The origin validation works correctly behind Cloudflare because the browser sends the public origin in both the HTTP Origin header and the clientData, and the RP ID hash verification uses the same ServerDomain value that was sent during credential creation, so they'll match.

Setting ServerDomain explicitly to the configured RP ID makes the system independent of the request host, which is crucial when operating behind a proxy or in hairpin scenarios — this aligns with the principle that the public origin is the single source of truth for all WebAuthn configuration.

Now I need to verify the metrics recording for the passkey sign-in path, since the framework's internal metrics aren't the same as the sign_ins_total counter that needs to be recorded with the passkey method. The existing RestaurantSignInManager avoids overriding SignInAsync to prevent double-counting on the password path, but for passkeys I'll need to override PasskeySignInAsync to capture the terminal outcome and audit it with the correct method label. The challenge is extracting the user identity from the passkey assertion so I can emit the security event.

I can't call the assertion twice since it's single-use (it consumes the TwoFactorUserIdScheme state cookie), so instead I'll call base.PasskeySignInAsync and then retrieve the authenticated user from Context.User on success using UserManager.GetUserAsync. On failure, there's no user to attribute since the assertion itself failed, so I'll just record the metric without a user reference, matching the behavior for unknown usernames in the password flow.

But wait — SignInAsync doesn't populate HttpContext.User synchronously within the same request; it only writes the auth cookie to the response. So reading Context.User after sign-in would still see the incoming anonymous principal, which won't work. I could bypass base.PasskeySignInAsync and compose the sign-in logic myself to capture the user from the assertion result, but that risks drifting from the framework's implementation. The cleanest approach is to have the page handle assertion explicitly and drive both the sign-in and audit from there.

Actually, let me trace through the framework's PasskeySignInCoreAsync flow: it performs the assertion to get the result with the user, runs the pre-sign-in check where our deactivation gate applies, updates the sign count, then calls SignInOrTwoFactorAsync which bypasses two-factor. I need to audit with the user, so I could override PasskeySignInAsync and re-resolve the user without re-running the assertion.

But that won't work since I won't have the user after base.PasskeySignInAsync succeeds. Alternatively, I could override the protected SignInOrTwoFactorAsync method since it receives the user, but that fires for password sign-ins and two-factor completions too, which would cause double-counting and require fragile logic to distinguish between paths. A cleaner approach might be to have the sign-in page itself call PasskeySignInAsync and then report the audit event afterward.

Actually, I think the best path is to override PasskeySignInAsync entirely and reimplement its core logic while adding auditing. The framework exposes PerformPasskeyAssertionAsync, UserManager.AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync, and the protected SignInOrTwoFactorAsync method, so I can call these same pieces from a subclass and inject the audit call at the right moment.

Implementing override logic... Completing the sign-in flow by calling the async method that handles the actual sign-in or two-factor authentication, then auditing the result before returning it. This approach mirrors the existing passkey sign-in logic exactly while ensuring proper auditing with the correct method identifier and avoiding redundant assertion validation.

The key challenge is that RecordMetric and AuditAsync currently hardcode the method type, so I need to refactor both to accept the method as a parameter — the password path will pass PasswordMethod while the passkey path passes PasskeyMethod. On assertion failure, there's no user subject available, so the metric gets recorded without a subject.

For the PreSignInCheck failure when a user is deactivated, the user exists, so I'll audit it as sign_in_failed with the passkey method. For AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync update failures, I'm leaning toward metric-only since it's an internal persistence issue rather than a credential rejection — treating it like the unknown-username case keeps the logic consistent.

Actually, I'm reconsidering: once we have the user object, every terminal result should go through the audit function with that user, result, and passkey method. This simplifies the code significantly — only the assertion-failed branch (no user found) stays metric-only, and everything after we have the user gets audited. That's cleaner and more consistent with the framework's semantics.

Now I'm thinking through the pages and enrollment flows. The spec says passkeys come first with password as a fallback option, and passkey enrollment is offered to guests after registration and sign-in as a dismissible nudge — always available but never required or gated. For this build scope, I need to cover the passkey registration and assertion flows, both username-first and discoverable, plus the full sign-in path.

To implement this, I need the DapperUserStore to implement IUserPasskeyStore<Person> with five methods, a migration to add the WebAuthn columns, wiring up the IPasskeyHandler<Person> and IdentityPasskeyOptions configuration with the RP ID and preference settings, an override in the sign-in manager for PasskeySignInAsync that handles auditing and the deactivation gate, and two endpoints — one for creation options that requires authentication and antiforgery, and one for request options that's anonymous-friendly for discoverable and username-first flows, also with antiforgery protection.

I'm also building a custom passkey-submit element in JavaScript and wrapping it in a Razor component, then adding a "Sign in with a passkey" button to the sign-in page that performs discoverable or username-first assertion and handles the credential JSON in the same static-SSR post, plus a /account/passkeys management page where users can list, add, delete, and potentially rename their passkeys.

For the passkey management surface, I'll keep it focused by combining list, add (with auto-generated default names), rename, and delete on a single page using inline rename forms per row rather than a separate route. Since there's no registration page yet in the current scope, the post-registration nudge doesn't have a home, but I could add a dismissible nudge after sign-in if needed for polish.

The sign-in page is static SSR, so I'll follow the template's pattern: a PasskeySubmit custom element that calls navigator.credentials, serializes the credential JSON as a form value via ElementInternals.setFormValue, and submits the form, with the server-side model binding the credential via [SupplyParameterFromForm].

For the JavaScript, I need to decide between a plain script tag loaded in App.razor or per-page versus a colocated .razor.js file. Colocated scripts require the enhanced navigation and JS module pipeline, which isn't wired up for static SSR pages without a Blazor circuit. The simplest approach is a plain <script src="js/passkey.js"> tag.

I'll take the template's custom element registration code and adapt it from a module format into a classic script that can be included globally. This way the custom element self-registers on page load and wires directly to the form without needing any Blazor JS infrastructure.

The custom element will read the antiforgery token attributes and post to the creation-options and request-options endpoints. I need to make sure the endpoint URLs and query parameter names (like username) match what the template expects, and I'll handle antiforgery validation the same way—manually calling ValidateRequestAsync and passing the token via request headers.

The creation-options endpoint requires authentication since you're registering a passkey for yourself, while request-options is anonymous for username-first or discoverable sign-in flows. The obligations middleware shouldn't interfere since those endpoints aren't pipeline pages, and anonymous requests to request-options bypass the middleware entirely. The middleware runs after auth but before antiforgery, so the endpoints can do their own antiforgery validation without conflict.

There's a potential ordering issue with the middleware pipeline that I need to check—

the obligations middleware comes before UseAntiforgery, but the passkey endpoints handle antiforgery manually anyway, so that should be fine. For authenticated users without obligations, creation-options works as expected.

I'm adding .RequireAuthorization() to creation-options. On the sign-in page, when the passkey button posts the form with the credential JSON, the server checks if Input.Passkey.CredentialJson is present and routes to either PasskeySignInAsync or the normal password flow.

The issue is that SignIn.razor uses OnValidSubmit with DataAnnotations that require username and password, but clicking the passkey button should bypass that validation. The template checks for the passkey credential first, but if validation fails before that check, OnValidSubmit never fires. I need to look at how the template actually structures the form to handle this.

The passkey button uses a custom element that intercepts the submit, prevents the default EditForm behavior, obtains the credential, and calls a native form submit that bypasses Blazor's validation entirely. This means the form posts with just the passkey credential, and on the server side, model binding populates Input.Passkey.CredentialJson before validation runs. But if username and password are marked as required and empty, the server-side validation would still fail.

Looking at how the template handles this — the LoginUser method is OnValidSubmit, which only fires if validation passes. For the passkey path to reach that method, either validation must have passed somehow, or there's something about the model setup that allows the passkey credential alone to satisfy the validation requirements.

The safest approach for my implementation is to separate concerns entirely: create a dedicated endpoint for passkey sign-in that doesn't go through the validated EditForm at all. This way the passkey credential JSON gets processed independently without needing to satisfy Email/Password validation constraints. The assertion challenge gets stored in a cookie by the request-options endpoint, then read back during sign-in, so the state persists across requests. I'm realizing the cleanest approach is to keep passkey sign-in as part of the same SignIn.razor page flow rather than splitting it into separate endpoints — that mirrors how password sign-in works and avoids the validation conflict. Let me check the existing Login.razor template to see how it's structured.

Show more
Done

Now I'm setting up the AccountEndpoints with the two passkey endpoints, using IAntiforgery for token validation and the UserManager/SignInManager for identity operations, returning JSON responses.

The creation-options endpoint requires authorization (using the default DenyAnonymous policy), while request-options is anonymous since no explicit policy is set. The ObligationsMiddleware running beforehand means authenticated users with obligations get redirected to the obligation page before reaching creation-options, which is fine since they shouldn't be managing passkeys mid-obligation anyway. Now I'm thinking through the request-options endpoint behavior...

For discoverable sign-in without a username, the endpoint passes null to the passkey request, which tells the browser to show all discoverable credentials. When a username is provided, it fetches that specific user's credentials instead. I'm setting the username field's autocomplete attribute to trigger the browser's conditional UI, and the JavaScript autofill logic will call request-options with the email and use conditional mediation to get credentials, posting on success. Writing the database migration... Adding the migration script...

I'm verifying that DbUp will discover and execute the new migration in the correct order by checking the lexicographic naming convention — the new script starting with "0002_" will naturally sort after "0001_initial_schema.sql", so the sequencing is correct.

The migration runner checks for pending scripts when IsUpToDate() is called, so on the next test run or application startup, the new migration will be applied to any database that's already had the initial schema applied.

Now I'm handling the update flow where I decode the credential ID and check if the action is to delete the passkey, removing it from the user's account if requested. Writing passkey rename logic...

I'm realizing there's an issue with how I'm handling the update — when I load the existing passkey and modify just the name, then call AddOrUpdatePasskeyAsync, the store is also updating fields like signature_counter, credential_display_name, is_backed_up, and is_user_verified, which could cause problems if those values have changed since I loaded them.

Actually, looking at what GetPasskeyAsync returns, it doesn't include the attestation data anyway since we don't store it, so the update path won't touch those columns — that's fine.

For the status message, I need to read it from the query string using a SupplyParameterFromQuery attribute and set it in OnInitializedAsync, then handle the redirect from the POST handler.

The redirect itself follows the POST-Redirect-GET pattern — NavigateTo in static SSR issues a 302 response and stops rendering, so I just need to return after calling it. But I'm wondering if NavigateTo actually works during an event handler in SSR or if I need to use a custom redirect manager pattern instead.

Actually, looking at the existing SignIn.razor code, it already calls NavigateTo in the submit handler and that works fine, so this approach is solid. Now I need to integrate the passkey button into SignIn.razor by switching to OnSubmit with EditContext and adding the passkey button alongside the nested model.

For validation, once I call Validate() on the EditContext, the DataAnnotationsValidator will populate the validation message store and they'll render properly. The nested Passkey model will bind correctly from the form since the PasskeySubmit component posts Input.Passkey.CredentialJson as a form field.

Now I'm adding the passkey script to App.razor before blazor.web.js loads, then adding a Passkeys link to MainLayout, and updating IdentityService.

For the service collection extensions, I need to register the PasskeyHandler and configure PasskeyOptions. The PasskeyHandler is public in Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity, as is PasskeyOptions, so I can add the handler registration after the SignInManager chain and before ConfigureApplicationCookie. Configuring the WebAuthn relying-party options with the server domain, user verification, and resident key requirements both set to preferred, then moving on to refactor the RestaurantSignInManager to handle audit logging and metrics more generically before adding a PasskeySignInAsync override, and finally adding the two new endpoints to AccountEndpoints.

Writing endpoint handlers... Finishing the response with the JSON content type, then setting up another endpoint for passkey request options that accepts an optional username parameter and returns the generated options.

I need to make sure the [FromQuery] attribute is available through the MVC using statement, which it is. The class documentation should be expanded to cover both sign-out and passkey option endpoints. Then I'll add the three route constants to AccountRoutes, verify that passkey pages aren't exempt from obligations enforcement (they shouldn't be), and write tests to confirm the passkey handler is properly registered in the dependency injection container. Now I'm setting up a new test file for the DapperUserStore passkey functionality, mirroring the existing test structure. I'll write tests that verify adding and retrieving passpkeys, finding owners by passkey ID, and ensuring updates only modify mutable fields like sign count and name while preserving immutable ones like the public key and creation timestamp.

I need to create a helper method that constructs UserPasskeyInfo objects with all the required fields—credential ID, public key, timestamps, sign count, transport methods, verification flags, and attestation data—then use GetPasskeysAsync and FindPasskeyAsync to verify the data persists correctly after updates and removals. I'll also need to create a test Person first using the store.

For the new test class, I'm setting up its own fixture with FixedClock, EphemeralDataProtectionProvider, and the connection factory with migrations, mirroring the pattern from DapperUserStoreTests. Since FixedClock is internal to the same namespace, it's accessible. The AddOrUpdate logic relies on FindPasskeyAsync to check if a passkey exists by credential ID before deciding whether to insert or update, and the UNIQUE constraint on credential_id enforces this at the database level.

RemovePasskeyAsync returns just Task (no value), and the integration test calls it directly then verifies the passkey is gone via GetPasskeysAsync. I also need to verify that FindByPasskeyIdAsync properly qualifies the person columns in the SELECT and uses the JOIN correctly.

Done
Let me verify the exact signatures of the protected members I'm calling in the passkey override.


Architected audit logging integration for passkey authentication flow
Architected audit logging integration for passkey authentication flow

Confirm signatures of PerformPasskeyAssertionAsync, SignInOrTwoFactorAsync, PreSignInCheck, PasskeySignInAsync

Read the framework PasskeySignInAsync core to confirm my override mirrors it exactly
I'm mirroring the exact flow of PasskeySignInCoreAsync with four sequential steps: performing the passkey assertion, running the pre-sign-in check, adding or updating the passkey, and then signing in while bypassing two-factor authentication.

The key difference is that I'm adding audit logging at each terminal point where the flow could fail or succeed. The base implementation wraps everything in try/catch to record framework metrics on exceptions, but my override replaces that entirely—exceptions just propagate naturally, which is fine since PerformPasskeyAssertionAsync already catches PasskeyException internally and returns a failure result rather than throwing.

One defensive detail: I'm checking that assertion.User and assertion.Passkey aren't null in addition to verifying Succeeded is true, even though the base assumes they're guaranteed non-null when success is true. That's just being extra safe. The one trade-off is that my override doesn't call the framework's internal metrics like _metrics.AuthenticateSignIn(...), but that's intentional—those are the framework's own telemetry separate from our custom audit logging.

Now I need to wire this up in the service collection extensions. Let me look at where the stamp validators are registered so I can add the passkey handler there.

Show more
Done
My override mirrors PasskeySignInCoreAsync exactly, adding only the audit calls. Now the DI wiring — let me find where the stamp validators are registered.

I'm adapting this into a classic script for the passkey functionality, updating the endpoint URLs to match my routes and adding a guard to prevent duplicate custom element registration while keeping the rest of the credential handling logic intact.

For the email parameter in the request URL, I need to handle the case where the email field might be empty — I'll extract it from the form data and use encodeURIComponent to properly encode the username, which handles special characters like + that could appear in email addresses. If the field is empty, I'll pass an empty string so the server treats it as a discoverable credential request.

The antiforgery token handling is already set up through the element's attributes — the server provides the header name and token value via IAntiforgery.GetTokens, and the fetch request includes it in the headers while the server validates it on each endpoint.

Done
Now the manage page. Let me check the existing EnrollTotp page to match this codebase's static-SSR form idioms and CSS classes.


Read EnrollTotp.razor to mirror page/form idioms
Read EnrollTotp.razor to mirror page/form idioms

Read EnrollTotp.razor to mirror page/form idioms
I'm seeing the patterns in this codebase now—the attribute decorators for routing and authorization, the semantic HTML structure with specific CSS classes for panels and typography, and the form components with their validation handlers and naming conventions.

For the Passkeys component, I need to follow the same EditForm pattern used elsewhere, wrapping the PasskeySubmit custom element inside an EditForm with a Model and FormName. The form binding will use SupplyParameterFromForm to capture the Input model, and the PasskeySubmit element will write its credential data into the form fields that EditForm manages.

For the per-row rename and delete forms, I'm considering whether to use EditForm or raw <form> elements. While the codebase favors EditForm, these per-row operations carry shared scalar parameters (Action, CredentialId, NewName) that don't fit a standard EditForm model pattern, so raw <form @formname> with antiforgery tokens and method="post" might be more appropriate here. I'll stick with plain forms for the per-row actions since they need individual form names, and use an EditForm for the add operation to keep things consistent with the existing patterns in the template.

Actually, let me reconsider—using plain <form> everywhere would be more uniform. The add form would bind the complex PasskeyInputModel through [SupplyParameterFromForm(FormName="add-passkey")], with the form name attribute and @onsubmit handler triggering the submission. This matches the template's approach exactly.

The key insight is that PasskeySubmit's native form.submit() call bypasses Blazor's enhanced navigation interception, forcing a real browser POST—which is what we want for SSR. The server then uses the _handler hidden field (generated from @formname) to route to the correct handler, not enhanced navigation. This works cleanly because SSR form handling is based on that _handler field, not on the enhanced nav system.

The delete and rename buttons are standard submit buttons that post with Action=delete or Action=rename, which the handler binds via [SupplyParameterFromForm]. These trigger normal submits (either enhanced or native), and the custom element only intercepts the passkey-specific submit.

Now I'm building the page structure to match the EnrollTotp visual layout, and I need to handle the credential ID round-tripping through the form.

The credential ID comes through as a Base64Url-encoded hidden input, then on post I decode it using Base64Url.DecodeFromChars, which takes a ReadOnlySpan<char> and returns a byte array (throwing FormatException if invalid). I should also add the [Authorize] attribute to the page since the app has no fallback auth policy and relies on the cookie's LoginPath redirect for unauthenticated users.

With [Authorize] in place, an anonymous user hitting the passkeys page gets redirected to sign-in. The user is null check in OnInitialized becomes nearly unreachable since authorization guarantees a principal, but I'll keep it as a safety guard since GetUserAsync could still return null if the user was deleted mid-session—that's the pattern the codebase follows.

Now I'm building the Passkeys.razor component, starting with the form for adding a new passkey.

Writing the passkey form...

I'm updating the SignIn.razor component to use an EditContext instead of the model binding approach, and I'll add a nested Passkey input model to the SignInInput class to support passkey authentication.

The submit handler will now fire on any submission, checking first if there's a passkey error or credential JSON present — if so, it calls PasskeySignInAsync and handles the result (Succeeded, IsLockedOut, IsNotAllowed, or generic failure). If neither passkey condition is met, it validates the form and proceeds with the password sign-in flow.
