I'm looking at the DapperUserStore constructor signature and its dependencies—it takes a connection factory, clock, identifier factory, data protection provider, and error describer. The FixedClock being used in tests is defined as internal somewhere in the test project, likely in a shared utilities file within the MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests.Identity namespace.

Now I'm setting up the passkey tests file with five test cases covering round-trip serialization, lookup by passkey ID, owner-specific retrieval, mutable field updates, and deletion. I need a helper method to construct UserPasskeyInfo objects with the right parameter order: credential ID, public key, creation timestamp, sign count, transports, user verification flags, attestation and client data. For the update test, I'll create a passkey with specific initial values and verify that only the mutable fields change. The update operation intentionally skips immutable fields like public_key, is_backup_eligible, and created_at, so they persist unchanged. Since FindPasskeyAsync queries by user and credentialId, the second AddOrUpdate correctly identifies the existing row and performs an update rather than inserting a duplicate. When reading back the PublicKey byte array and CreatedAt DateTimeOffset, the stored timestamptz value returns with UTC offset alignment.

Postgres timestamptz has microsecond precision while DateTimeOffset uses 100-nanosecond ticks, but since the test clock is fixed at whole seconds (2026-01-15 12:00:00), the round-trip comparison works exactly. I'll assert that the retrieved createdAt matches the original T value by comparing via UtcDateTime to sidestep any offset representation quirks. I need to verify how Npgsql and Dapper handle the conversion from PostgreSQL's timestamptz to DateTimeOffset. Npgsql can read timestamptz directly as DateTimeOffset with a UTC offset, so when Dapper maps the result to my row type, it should preserve that conversion properly.

Looking at the existing codebase, the Person entity already uses DateTimeOffset for fields like CreatedAt and LockoutEndAt, and those round-trip correctly through the store. So using DateTimeOffset for PasskeyCredentialRow.CreatedAt is consistent with what's already working.

For the test, xUnit will compare byte arrays by value through IEnumerable, so the assertion for PublicKey should work fine. I'm going to write the test class using TestContext.Current.CancellationToken like the existing tests do, and I'll add a helper to create a second person for testing the null case. But I need to think through the fixture setup — if each test class gets its own PostgreSqlFixture instance, that could affect how the container is shared across tests. anted sharing they'd have a collection. PostgreSqlFixture should be safe to instantiate per-class since it just manages container lifecycle, and xUnit will initialize the fixture's IAsyncLifetime before the test class runs, so the ConnectionString will be available when needed.

Now I'm checking if there's already a collection definition I can reuse to avoid spinning up duplicate containers.

Now I'm checking the Dapper methods I'm calling: ExecuteAsync, QueryAsync<T>, and QuerySingleOrDefaultAsync<T> with CommandDefinition—all of which are already used in the existing store. The question is whether Dapper can map to properties with init setters. At the C# language level, init setters are restricted to object initializers and constructors, but at the IL level they're just normal setters, so Dapper's reflection-based property setting should work fine.

To stay consistent with the codebase style though, I should check how existing Dapper-mapped types like Person declare their properties—whether they use init or set—and match that convention.

Done
I've completed the core implementation of the M2 passkeys slice. Here's where things stand.

What I built
Every framework assumption was verified against the actual .NET 10 ASP.NET Core source before writing code. Three findings shaped the design:

AddIdentityCore does not register IPasskeyHandler (only the monolithic AddIdentity does) — so I register PasskeyHandler<Person> explicitly, exactly as this codebase already does for the stamp validators.
WebAuthn assertion reads the stored IsBackupEligible bit (step 19) and fails the ceremony on mismatch — but the spec's passkey_credential table has no column for it. This is precisely the framework-gap BUILD_PROGRESS told me to check for, so I added a small additive migration.
PasskeySignInAsync already bypasses two-factor (bypassTwoFactor: true) and runs PreSignInCheck — so §3.5's "passkey never gets a TOTP challenge" and the deactivation gate both hold by construction. My override only adds auditing with method=passkey, mirroring the framework's core exactly.
Files changed (11) and added (6)
New:

0002_passkey_credential_webauthn_state.sql — adds 3 boolean columns (additive, DEFAULT false)
Identity/PasskeyContracts.cs — PasskeyOperation enum + PasskeyInputModel
Components/Account/PasskeySubmit.razor — the ceremony button + antiforgery header
Components/Account/Pages/Passkeys.razor — list/add/rename/remove management page
wwwroot/js/passkey.js — the passkey-submit custom element
DapperUserStorePasskeyTests.cs — 8 store round-trip tests
Changed: DapperUserStore.cs (implements IUserPasskeyStore), RestaurantSignInManager.cs (passkey audit override), IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs (handler + options), AccountEndpoints.cs (two options endpoints), ObligationsEnforcement.cs (3 route constants), SignIn.razor (OnSubmit + passkey button), App.razor, MainLayout.razor, app.css, IdentityWiringTests.cs (3 new facts).

Verified
Migration auto-embeds and runs after 0001; _identifierFactory.Create(), OpenAsync, Dapper usage, SupportsUserPasskey, SecurityEventType constants, RecordAsync signature, _Imports coverage, and the ResolveWebAuthnRelyingPartyId() → "localhost" wiring all check out.

My new migration adds columns, not a table, so a table-existence test is safe — but you should glance at that test if it asserts an exact script count.
No dotnet build was possible here (no SDK/NuGet), so this is verified by careful static analysis, not compilation.
No files need deleting. I have not yet built the tarball or updated BUILD_PROGRESS.md / the spec's §8.2 DDL (the schema deviation should be recorded there). Say the word and I'll package everything into the structured tarball and produce those doc updates.

Locate the passkey_credential DDL and §8.2 heading in the repo spec copy

Read the exact passkey_credential DDL block
I'm weighing whether to keep the DDL as the original verbatim schema or add the columns that came through migration 0002. The spec says the DDL ships exactly as 0001, so adding those columns would break that contract. I could either preserve the DDL in its original form and note the augmentation separately, or modify the DDL itself—but that second option violates the verbatim requirement.

The best path forward is to leave the DDL block untouched (keeping the "ships verbatim as 0001" statement true) and insert a note right after the passkey_credential table documenting what 0002 added. This way the spec remains the authoritative schema record while being explicit about which migration introduced which columns. I need to preserve the contract that §8.2's DDL documents 0001 exactly as it was journaled, so I shouldn't edit that code block. Instead, I'll add a note right after the closing code fence explaining that the passkey_credential table was extended in the 0002 migration, keeping the normative DDL untouched while still documenting the schema evolution.

I'm verifying the usage patterns in RestaurantSignInManager — checking that Results.Content with JSON content type is available, and confirming the metrics recording still works with the existing _metrics.RecordSignIn method. I'm switching the EditForm to use EditContext instead of the Model binding, and removing the Model attribute. The passkey button posts Input.Passkey.CredentialJson, which works because Passkey is a nullable complex property that Blazor SSR model binding will instantiate when needed. On password-only posts, Input.Passkey stays null so the null-coalescing checks handle it gracefully.

Now I'm verifying that the Classify method exists and checking that RestaurantSignInManager compiles correctly. I'm also confirming the antiforgery setup in PasskeySubmit.razor — the GetService extension, AntiforgeryTokenSet, and IAntiforgery are all properly imported and available.

Investigated missing namespace import and implicit usings dependency
Investigated missing namespace import and implicit usings dependency

Check System.Linq import and brace/paren balance across the C# files
I'm noticing that System.Linq isn't explicitly imported in DapperUserStore.cs, but I'm using LINQ methods like Select and ToList in GetPasskeysAsync. The code might still compile if the project has implicit usings enabled, which would automatically include System.Linq, but I should verify whether the existing code already relies on LINQ elsewhere to confirm this assumption.

Done
All balanced. The file lacks an explicit using System.Linq for my .Select().ToList() — let me confirm ImplicitUsings is enabled (which would provide it, and explains why Task/IList need no explicit using either).


Verified namespace imports and property configurations across project files
Verified namespace imports and property configurations across project files

Confirm ImplicitUsings enabled and target framework
With ImplicitUsings enabled, all the standard namespaces like System.Linq, System.Collections.Generic, and System.Threading.Tasks are automatically available, so methods like Select and ToList work without explicit using statements. Types like Uri, DateTimeOffset, Guid, and Task all resolve through these implicit usings across my files. Now I'm checking whether System.Buffers.Text.Base64Url is covered by the implicit usings or if I need to handle it separately.

System.Buffers.Text isn't in the default implicit usings set, so I correctly added @using System.Buffers.Text in Passkeys.razor. For CancellationToken, the implicit usings already include System.Threading and System.Threading.Tasks, so that's covered. In PasskeySubmit.razor, Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection isn't included by default in Razor implicit usings, so I added that using statement as well. For AccountEndpoints.cs, the web SDK project automatically includes Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http, and Microsoft.AspNetCore.Routing in its implicit usings, so Results, IEndpointRouteBuilder, MapPost, and HttpContext all resolve without explicit using statements.

Since the original code compiled successfully with only using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity; using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc; using MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity;, I know the web implicit usings cover those ASP.NET Core types. I did add IAntiforgery explicitly since Microsoft.AspNetCore.Antiforgery isn't part of the implicit usings. Now I need to verify the WebApplication project setup. I need to verify the property names on PasskeyUserEntity match what I'm using — Id, Name, and DisplayName as strings.

Done
The M2 passkeys slice is complete and packaged. Extract the tarball at your repo root (tar xzf myrestaurant-passkey-slice.tar.gz) — every path inside is repo-relative and overwrites in place.

Files to delete
None. This slice is purely additive/edits.

What's in the tarball — 19 files
6 new:

src/MyRestaurant.DataAccess/Migrations/0002_passkey_credential_webauthn_state.sql
src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/Identity/PasskeyContracts.cs
src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/Components/Account/PasskeySubmit.razor
src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/Components/Account/Pages/Passkeys.razor
src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/wwwroot/js/passkey.js
tests/MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Tests/Identity/DapperUserStorePasskeyTests.cs
13 changed (full files): DapperUserStore.cs, RestaurantSignInManager.cs, IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs, AccountEndpoints.cs, ObligationsEnforcement.cs, SignIn.razor, App.razor, MainLayout.razor, app.css, IdentityWiringTests.cs, and the three docs (BUILD_PROGRESS.md, TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION.md, DOCUMENTATION_REVIEW.md).

The three decisions worth knowing
A migration was required (0002). .NET 10's UserPasskeyInfo carries WebAuthn state the spec's passkey_credential table didn't model, and assertion reads the stored backup-eligible bit and fails the ceremony on a mismatch — so it must persist. 0002 adds three booleans (additive, DEFAULT false); 0001 is untouched. This is the framework gap §3.3 anticipated; no fallback library was needed. Recorded in the spec's §8.2 schema-evolution note and as ledger row F-34.
The passkey handler is registered by hand. AddIdentityCore (what this app uses) doesn't register IPasskeyHandler — only AddIdentity does — so I register PasskeyHandler<Person> explicitly, exactly as the codebase already does for the stamp validators. A wiring test guards it.
The passkey sign-in never gets a TOTP challenge, by construction. My PasskeySignInAsync override reproduces the framework's assertion → pre-check → sign-in core (inheriting its bypassTwoFactor: true) and adds only the central auditing (method=passkey).
Before you trust it
I have no .NET SDK here, so this is verified by reading the .NET 10 framework source (every type, signature, and result-shape confirmed) plus brace/paren and import checks — not by compiling. Per the usual pattern, run dotnet build / dotnet test; the three passkey Razor components are the likeliest spot for anything a compiler catches. Manual test must be on your stable named-tunnel domain, since passkeys bind to the RP ID (quick tunnels won't keep them).

One deferred item, by design: the §3.3 post-registration passkey nudge has no home yet (there's no registration page until /setup / M3), so only the durable Passkeys management page and the sign-in button ship now. Noted in BUILD_PROGRESS.


Myrestaurant passkey slice.tar
GZ
